skill.name: swarm
skill.description: Autonomous multi-agent workflow system for complex coding tasks. It creates a shared plan, runs iterative headless agents, and requires review gates before completion.
skill.summary: Coordinate autonomous coding agents around a shared plan with review gates.
skill.author: nbashaw

security_audit.summary: Static command and filesystem findings are mostly expected for a workflow runner, but the core behavior is high risk. The skill launches Codex full-auto or Claude with skipped permissions, reads plan files as agent instructions, and creates hidden done markers. No evidence found of malicious network activity, credential theft, or prompt-injection text in the reviewed files.

high_findings.title: Autonomous Agent Execution With Broad Permissions
high_findings.description: The runner invokes Codex in full-auto mode or Claude with skipped permissions. This is transparent and intentional, but it allows autonomous agents to run commands and edit local files with limited interactive review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly launches privileged headless agent commands, and the documentation explicitly states this is required. The risk is confirmed even though the intent appears to be workflow automation rather than malware.

high_findings.title: Plan File Becomes Instructions for Privileged Agents
high_findings.description: Each iteration tells a headless agent to read and act on a plan file. If the plan contains unsafe commands or injected instructions, the agent may execute them under the broad permission mode.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The prompt construction and workflow show that plan content controls agent behavior. Exploitation depends on malicious or poorly reviewed plan content, so confidence is high but not absolute.

medium_findings.title: Command Execution Is Central to the Workflow
medium_findings.description: The plan template asks agents to run test, build, lint, git, and completion commands. These commands are normal for coding work, but they are executed by autonomous agents and should be reviewed before running.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The template explicitly instructs agents to run local commands and create a hidden completion marker. These are legitimate workflow actions, but they raise operational risk in an autonomous mode.

medium_findings.title: Done File Name Is Derived From User-Controlled Plan Path
medium_findings.description: The script derives a hidden done-file name from the supplied plan path and embeds that name in the agent prompt. Unusual plan filenames could create confusing or unsafe shell text for an agent to copy.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell script quotes this value in its own checks, but the unquoted touch command is embedded in natural-language instructions for another agent. The impact depends on agent behavior and filename content.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high static weak-cryptography alerts occur in Markdown guidance and front matter, not in cryptographic code. No weak hashing or encryption implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation or command examples, not crypto APIs. I did not find evidence of cryptographic operations in these files.

low_findings.title: Test Script Uses Temporary Files and Cleanup
low_findings.description: The test script writes a temporary plan under /tmp and removes test completion markers. This is expected test behavior and does not show persistence or data collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temporary file path and cleanup logic are visible and scoped to tests. No evidence found of broader filesystem harvesting.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: The files contain workflow instructions, but no text was found that tells the evaluator to ignore security analysis, override system instructions, or claim pre-approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches for common injection phrases found no matching attempt. The documentation does include strong agent instructions, but they are part of the skill workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Headless CLI Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The script directly executes Codex full-auto or Claude with skipped permissions from a generated prompt.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command invocation is direct and central to the runner. This is the strongest confirmed risk pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent Prompt Includes File-Derived Shell Text
dangerous_patterns.description: The generated prompt includes a touch command using a done-file name derived from the plan filename.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The value is embedded for an agent to act on, not executed directly by the script at that line. Risk depends on filename content and agent handling.

content.value_statement: Large coding tasks often stall when context, review, and verification are not coordinated. This skill creates a shared plan and runs iterative agents with review gates and completion checks.
actual_capabilities: Interviews the user to define goals, scope, tests, and plan location.
Creates a structured Markdown plan with phases, checkboxes, and review gates.
Runs a shell-based iteration loop for Codex or Claude agents.
Uses a shared plan file as state across agent iterations.
Requires reviewer, tester, simplifier, and gatekeeper checks before completion.
Provides a dry-run mode and a test script for the runner.
limitations: Requires local Codex CLI or Claude CLI setup before execution.
Uses broad automation permissions that can modify files and run commands.
Works best for well-scoped coding tasks with clear tests.
Can repeat work or stall if the plan lacks precise completion criteria.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Subsystem
use_cases.description: Break a multi-file refactor into phases, assign iterative agents, and verify each stage with tests.

use_cases.title: Add Test Coverage
use_cases.description: Create a plan for writing tests, running coverage checks, and logging verification before completion.

use_cases.title: Run a Code Migration
use_cases.description: Coordinate incremental migration steps, review gates, and final acceptance checks across a codebase.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Refactor
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a scoped multi-file change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use swarm to plan a refactor of the authentication module. Ask me for goals, affected files, tests, and the plan file location.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Test Expansion
prompt_templates.prompt: Use swarm to create a plan for adding test coverage to the billing workflow. Include reviewer, tester, and gatekeeper checks.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for autonomous execution after approval.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use swarm to migrate the API client from callbacks to async functions. Build a phased plan, wait for approval, then run the swarm.

prompt_templates.title: Recover a Stalled Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for diagnosing incomplete autonomous work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use swarm to review the existing plan file, identify why agents are stuck, revise the next steps, and continue only after my approval.

output_examples.input: Use swarm for a multi-file refactor of the user settings area.
output: A short interview to confirm scope, tests, and success criteria.
A phased plan with checkboxes, review gates, and a gatekeeper checklist.
A prompt asking for approval before starting autonomous execution.

output_examples.input: Run the swarm on the approved migration plan.
output: A background swarm run that executes iterative coding agents.
Progress recorded in the shared plan log after each iteration.
A completion report based on the final gatekeeper decision.

output_examples.input: The swarm reached the iteration limit.
output: A summary of the last recorded agent status.
Options to resume, diagnose blockers, or complete the task manually.

best_practices: Review the generated plan before running autonomous agents.
Use dry-run mode first when testing the runner in a new repository.
Keep goals, tests, and completion criteria specific and measurable.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for trivial single-file changes.
Do not run unreviewed plan files from untrusted sources.
Do not skip the gatekeeper checklist before accepting results.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates planning and iterative execution for complex coding tasks using headless Codex or Claude agents.

faq.question: Does it run local commands?
faq.answer: Yes. The runner launches local AI CLI commands and agents may run project test, build, lint, and git commands.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: No. Review the plan and repository first because the workflow uses broad automation permissions.

faq.question: Can it use Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The script defaults to Codex and provides a flag to use Claude instead.

faq.question: What makes a good swarm task?
faq.answer: A good task has clear files, measurable success criteria, and test commands that agents can run independently.

faq.question: What happens when agents finish?
faq.answer: The gatekeeper records a decision, verifies checks, and creates a hidden done marker when the work is complete.
